text.title: API-First Toolchain
text.summary: The complete API-first workflow: spec → Spectral lint → Prism mock → OpenAPI Generator stubs → schemathesis contract tests. Tool roles and agent prompt patterns for each phase.

list.item: DESIGN — Write OpenAPI 3.1 spec (Stoplight Studio, Swagger Editor, or text editor)
VALIDATE — Spectral lint: spectral lint openapi.yaml --ruleset .spectral.yaml
MOCK — Prism mock server: prism mock openapi.yaml --port 4010 --dynamic
IMPLEMENT — Generate stubs: openapi-generator-cli generate -i openapi.yaml -g python-fastapi -o ./generated
TEST — Contract tests: schemathesis run openapi.yaml --url http://localhost:8000

rule.statement: Start Prism with --dynamic flag to generate random data from schema constraints when no explicit example is provided. Without --dynamic, missing examples produce empty responses.
rule.rationale: Frontend teams building against the mock need realistic data shapes. --dynamic generates values that match schema types, formats, and constraints even without explicit examples.

example.code: # Start mock server (lint first) spectral lint openapi.yaml --ruleset .spectral.yaml prism mock openapi.yaml --port 4010 --dynamic & echo "Mock server running at http://localhost:4010" echo "Docs at http://localhost:4010/__docs"

rule.statement: Run schemathesis or dredd against the live implementation in CI. When a contract test fails, determine whether the spec or the implementation is correct — never auto-fix the spec to match a buggy implementation.
rule.rationale: Contract tests enforce that the implementation matches its documented contract. Automatically updating the spec to match implementation failures defeats the purpose of the spec as the source of truth.

rule.statement: When generating implementation code from spec, pass the full OpenAPI spec as context and instruct the LLM to use operationId values as function names. For APIs with 100+ endpoints, work on one tag group at a time to avoid context window overflow.
rule.rationale: operationId creates a traceable mapping from spec to code. Breaking large specs by tag group keeps the context manageable while maintaining the spec as the grounding source.
